practices: The course framework is built on three core Science Practices. Practice 1, Creating Representations (20-35% of FRQ), involves creating diagrams, graphs, and models to depict physical phenomena. Practice 2, Mathematical Routines (55-75% of MCQ, 30-40% of FRQ), involves conducting analyses to derive, calculate, estimate, or predict physical quantities. Practice 3, Scientific Questioning and Argumentation (25-35% of MCQ, 35-45% of FRQ), focuses on creating experimental procedures, analyzing data, and supporting claims with evidence and physical principles.

keyTopics: Kinetic Theory of Temperature and Pressure
Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics (qualitative)
Electric Charge and Electric Force (Coulomb's Law)
Conservation of Electric Charge and the Process of Charging
Electric Fields and Electric Field Lines
Electric Potential Energy of a System
Electric Potential due to point charges
Physical properties and capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor
Equivalent resistance for series and parallel circuits
Magnetic fields produced by current-carrying wires
Reflection of light and the Law of Reflection
Images Formed by Mirrors (plane, convex, concave)
Refraction of light and Snell’s Law
Ray diagrams for mirrors and lenses
Properties of Wave Pulses and Waves (transverse vs. longitudinal)
Wave Interference, Superposition, and Standing Waves
Quantum Theory and Wave-Particle Duality (de Broglie wavelength)
The Bohr Model of the Atom and atomic energy levels
Types of Radioactive Decay (alpha, beta, gamma) and half-life

examFormat.freeResponse: 4 questions (1 Mathematical Routines, 1 Translation Between Representations, 1 Experimental Design and Analysis, 1 Qualitative/Quantitative Translation), 100 minutes, 50% of total score

antiPatterns: The course explicitly limits the scope of several topics. Students are not expected to mathematically model charging/discharging RC circuits over time, only describe initial and final states. Treatment of the Doppler effect and the second law of thermodynamics is qualitative only. Magnetic force calculations on moving charges are limited to angles of 0, 90, and 180 degrees. Students are not expected to know the functional form of the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. Analysis of electron structure is limited to energy levels, not orbitals or probability functions. Students are not expected to memorize specific isotopic decay processes or half-lives. Calculations of electric force/potential are limited to configurations of four or fewer point charges.
